Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often try to work through problems physically or practice concepts in real-world situations rather than just reading. I'd usually carve out chunks of time when I could, maybe 3-4 hours at a time a few days a week, but it wasn't super consistent. My attendance wasn't great because I found I could sometimes learn better on my own, working through materials at my own pace.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really focused and study regularly, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was approaching. I have extracurriculars that take up time, so I had to fit studying around those commitments. When anxiety hit hard, sometimes my schedule would completely fall apart and I'd have to scramble to catch up.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if grades had come back. Sometimes I'd log in because a classmate mentioned something in our group chat and I wanted to see what they were talking about. Deadlines were definitely the biggest motivator to get me logging in regularly. I'd also check discussion boards occasionally to see if anyone had questions similar to mine.
Q4: Yeah, there were stretches where checking the LMS became more automatic, especially mid-semester when things got busy. I'd check it in the morning while eating breakfast, just a quick scroll to see what was happening. The peer support group I was part of helped with that because we'd remind each other about stuff, so logging in became part of staying connected with them. It wasn't perfectly consistent though.
Q5: The video content was actually really helpful for me, which is why I watched over 100 minutes of it. I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. The discussion posts were useful too because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. Anything hands-on or interactive felt more valuable than just reading text.
Q6: I'll admit I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and some of the optional materials. If something felt too theoretical or text-heavy, I'd skim it at best. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so walls of text just don't stick for me the same way. Some resources felt redundant too, like they were covering the same thing in a less engaging format.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something practical or if it would directly help with an assignment or exam. I also valued things that let me interact with peers because that social element kept me motivated. If something felt like busywork or didn't have clear relevance, I'd deprioritize it quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my anxiety levels being what they are, I had to triage constantly and focus on what was required first. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff because I knew it might help, but the required work always took priority. When I did do optional activities, it was usually because a peer recommended them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked with them offline, especially PDFs and practice problems. I'd print things out and work through them by hand because that's how I learn best. I also used YouTube videos and external resources to supplement what was in the course. My notes were all handwritten in physical notebooks that obviously don't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I was actively studying but it wouldn't show in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper, discussing concepts with study partners in person, or practicing applications of what we learned. Sometimes I'd have a PDF open for five minutes in the LMS but then spend two hours working through it offline. The system only saw that brief login.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then never access them through the LMS again even though I was reviewing them multiple times. It made more sense to have everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the LMS interface. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than my access logs would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my anxiety spiked. Sometimes I'd feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do and just freeze up. When multiple deadlines piled up or when I was dealing with personal stuff, engaging with the course felt impossible. The surprise of unexpected assignments or changes to the schedule really threw me off too.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed from other responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd log in, maybe skim something, but not really absorb it. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I sometimes had to choose between course work and other commitments. Confusion about assignments also made me hesitant to engage until I could get clarity from peers.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd have anxiety about something unrelated and just not be able to focus. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't absorbed anything and would have to go back later. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually present mentally.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on things that felt overwhelming or unclear. If an assignment seemed too big or I wasn't sure how to start, I'd put it off even knowing it was important. My anxiety would build up around certain tasks, making them harder to approach. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because putting my thoughts out there publicly felt intimidating.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, sometimes cramming a lot of work into a short period. My login frequency probably spiked around due dates. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it's how I managed to get things done given everything else going on.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more. Knowing what I was doing right or wrong helped me focus my efforts better. But sometimes feedback came too late to really change my approach for the next assignment. The peer support helped a lot here because we'd share feedback with each other and discuss it.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When things were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines and expectations, I could plan better. But when the structure was loose or unclear, my anxiety would increase and I'd disengage. Having eight online courses meant I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, and clearer structures made that easier.
Q19: Flexible deadlines or understanding instructors made engagement easier because I didn't feel as much pressure when anxiety hit. The peer interaction opportunities were great for me since I learn well from others. What made it harder was when there was too much reading-heavy content without interactive elements, or when expectations weren't clearly communicated upfront.
Q20: I think students might be searching for information, trying to understand what's expected, or just feeling lost. High activity could mean someone is struggling and looking for help rather than actually making progress. They might also be anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Sometimes you're active because you're confused, not because you're productive.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. My attendance was low and my LMS activity was moderate, but I still passed. I did a lot of learning offline through hands-on practice and peer discussions. The LMS doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials or learning that happens in study groups. Performance doesn't always correlate with platform activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active or how often you engage with certain materials. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're anxiously refreshing the page or deeply focused on learning. They also miss all the offline work, peer conversations, and the emotional state behind your actions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, engagement is when I'm actively thinking about concepts, discussing them with peers, and trying to apply them. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to get through this course, or how my anxiety affected my engagement patterns. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or practicing concepts physically. It also wouldn't show the resilience it took to keep going when things got hard, or the genuine learning that happened outside the digital space.
